title: Share of one-year-olds vaccinated against measles
subtitle: Share of one-year-olds who have received the first dose of the measles vaccine (MCV1).
caption:
Share of one-year-olds vaccinated against measles, 2024

Share of one-year-olds who have received the first dose of the measles vaccine (MCV1).

This choropleth map displays the percentage of one-year-olds who received the first dose of the measles vaccine in 2024, using a continuous color scale ranging from light yellow for 0% coverage to dark blue for 100% coverage. The highest vaccination rates, represented by the darkest blue shades indicating 95% to 100% coverage, appear across most of North America, Europe, and large parts of Asia including China, Russia, and India. In South America, Brazil and Argentina fall within this highest band. Moderate coverage levels, shown in medium blue tones corresponding to the 70% to 90% range, characterize much of Southeast Asia, parts of South America like Peru and Colombia, and scattered nations in Eastern Europe and the Middle East. Lower vaccination rates, visible as light green and yellow-green colors representing the 40% to 60% band, concentrate heavily across sub-Saharan Africa, including countries like Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The lowest coverage levels, falling in the 0% to 40% range and shown in pale yellow, appear in a few specific African nations such as Somalia and South Sudan. Greenland and some small island territories show no data, marked with diagonal hatching. The metric measures the share of one-year-olds who have received the first dose of the measles vaccine. Measles is a highly contagious viral disease, most common in young children, with effects including blindness, inflammation of the brain, severe diarrhea, and severe respiratory infections such as pneumonia. The geographic pattern shows high vaccination coverage across most of the world's most populous regions, with the lowest rates concentrated in specific areas of sub-Saharan Africa.
